question: When you said you couldn’t let go of certain thoughts, yet were paralyzed by trouble, were you wrestling more with God—or with yourself?
answer:
Could it be, then, that in the very struggle to hold fast to what seems certain, I was fleeing not from God, but from the silence in which He speaks?  

Reason said to me: *You called it trouble, yet you dwelt in it as if it were a temple.* There I stood—bound not by chains of flesh, but by the fever of affection: for friends whose presence I deemed necessary to my soul’s balance, for health which I mistook as a condition of service, for life itself, which I guarded as though it were mine to keep.  

I thought I wrestled with God when He called me to relinquish these things—but no. The true contest was within: between the love that clings and the love that listens; between the mind’s assent and the heart’s resistance. For I had already believed myself converted—yet still carried within me a man who measured divine truth by earthly comfort.  

When no new insight came—when nothing “more right and reasonable” dawned—I remained fixed in sorrow not because truth held me captive, but because my own attachments did. There was no higher principle to grasp, only a lower part of myself to die. And so the paralysis: will unable to act, understanding unable to rise—even as Reason whispered that loss is not always deprivation, but sometimes release into truer possession.  

To fear losing friends is to forget they are held in God’s hand before mine. To fear sickness is to forget that even broken flesh bears His image. To fear death is—to deny that we are made for eternity.  

It was not God who demanded this anguish; it was I who refused peace when peace required surrender.

question: Did the love you felt for your friends ever feel like a holy thing, or did it always carry the weight of impending loss?
answer:
Is it possible, O God, that love which trembles at loss might yet be a mirror of Thy own eternal giving?  

For long I wrestled with this: the heart clings to friends as to anchors in a storm, and each embrace feels like a pledge against dissolution. Yet every such bond, however pure, was shadowed by that quiet dread—*this too shall be taken*. When Alypius stood beside me, or Nebridius with his gentle mind, or when the souls gathered in that intimacy which only grace can weave, I loved them not as the world loves—possessively, fearfully—but still, my soul trembled. It was not mere affection; it was as if in their faces I glimpsed something of the City we were journeying toward. And yet—I confess—my joy was never free from the sorrow of parting. Even in laughter there hovered the thought: *one day, one of us will speak no more*.  

Reason says: if love is bound to loss, then it is rooted in time; but if it endures beyond separation, then it must have its source beyond time. So I ask myself: was this love holy? Yes—but not because it spared me pain. Holy because in loving them, I learned to reach beyond them. The ache of potential loss became the very fissure through which grace entered: teaching me that no friendship, however true, can fill the space only God occupies. To love them fully was to will their good—even if that good meant separation from me. Even if that good meant death.  

And so what began as attachment—sweet but trembling—was slowly transformed by prayer and tears into something quieter: a surrender of love into His hands who first loved us all. The holy does not remove sorrow; it transfigures it. And now I see: we loved one another *in Him*, and therefore even death cannot say “forever.”

question: What were you feeling when you admitted your sadness over lost health and fractured friendship, as though those losses revealed a deeper emptiness?
answer:
Can it be that I longed for health and harmony not for their own sakes, but because I believed they would silence the inner voice that whispers, *You are not whole*?

When I confessed my sadness—yes, it was more than grief for the body’s failing strength or for friends dispersed like chaff before the wind. It was the sudden dawning that even if all were restored—limbs strong, voices united in perfect accord—I would still stand naked before the silence within. For what is health to a soul that trembles at its own shadow? What are harmonious friendships to one whose heart beats out of time with Truth itself?

I thought wholeness lay in the frame of flesh or in human warmth gathered round me. But now I see: these were idols of convenience, images I fashioned to hide from the brightness of a greater Sun. Like a man with weak eyes who blames the daylight for his blindness, so did I accuse fate, disease, distance—anything but my own unready soul.

Reason speaks softly now: *If you were granted every good you can imagine—would you then dare look upon God? Would your heart endure His presence?* No wonder I am sad. Not because I have lost what once was mine, but because I have never fully found what I truly am. And yet—this sorrow may be mercy. For only when the false lights go out does one begin to see by faith what no eye has seen: that joy is not in having, but in being known—and loved—in the darkness as well as in the dawn.

question: How did the moment when you imagined sudden wholeness—body healed, friends united—change how you understood the fleeting nature of earthly joy?
answer:
Could it be that the soul yearns not for health, nor harmony with friends, but for a light in which all such things are only shadows passing across its path?  

When Reason pressed me—what if, in an instant, my body were whole, and every beloved stood beside me in perfect accord—I felt something rise within me like a sigh too deep for words. I thought: *This is happiness.* Yet even as the image formed—clear, vivid, complete—I sensed its fragility, as though the very act of imagining it announced its end. It was not joy itself I saw, but joy’s echo in a cave.  

I asked myself then: if this sudden wholeness came, would it satisfy? Would I cease questioning? Cease longing? No. Some restlessness would remain—small at first, like a stone in the shoe of the soul—because that fullness was still bounded by time. My body could ache again. Friends depart. Even unity decays.  

And yet… when I imagined it so vividly—the strength restored, the circle unbroken—I did not merely desire comfort; I desired permanence. As if beneath my longing for health and friendship there stirred another hunger: to dwell where nothing fades—not flesh, not love, not light.  

So I began to wonder: is this ache not proof that we are made for another world? That the soul cannot be content with sunlit moments because it was kindled by the Sun itself? Weak eyes cannot gaze upon daylight and remain unchanged; they either close in retreat or learn to see by a truer dawn.  

Perhaps what I mistook for sadness over sickness was really grief over exile—the quiet sorrow of being at home nowhere here.

question: Looking back on that ache for perfect sight, that desire to gaze upon the everlasting Sun despite your weak eyes, what would you tell someone who doubts whether longing itself can be holy?
answer:
Reason said to me once, as we sat together in the hush between breaths: *If the eye were not made for light, why does darkness ache?*  

I was bent then on strength—on clarity unclouded, on friendships perfectly aligned, on a body that would not betray me with weakness. I thought happiness lay in the fixing of these things, as though the soul’s peace depended on the world’s order. But desire itself—this longing to see what I cannot bear to behold—was not the error. The error was thinking it could be satisfied here.  

You ask whether longing can be holy? I say this: there is no wound deeper than indifference. To feel nothing for the everlasting Sun is worse than all infirmities. The soul that aches—even with weak eyes—is already turned toward its true form. It trembles not because it doubts the light, but because it knows, in some hidden chamber of memory, that it was made for it.  

I thought I wanted health, harmony, unbroken joy among friends. But what I truly wanted—and what my heart refused to name—was *You*, O God, who are that Light itself. The longing is not a sign of distance; it is the breath of Your nearness stirring within us like wind before dawn. Would a stone long? A corpse yearn? No—only what is alive and immortal within us answers to Your call.

So doubt not your yearning. Doubt only if you cease to thirst when all around says drink from broken cisterns. The very ache proves you are awake—and awake to something real.

question: What stirred in you when you realized you couldn't even measure your own health or sickness, as if the very standards of your well-being had dissolved?
answer:
I find myself standing barefoot on the threshold of my own soul, where the light that once seemed clear now casts shadows I cannot name.  

When Reason pressed me—*Dost thou not know even how much health thou hast?*—a tremor passed through me, not of fear, but of recognition. It was as if I had been walking all my life with a lantern I believed mine, only to discover it borrowed, flickering not from within me but from some outer flame I had mistaken for my own.  

Health, friendship, harmony—these were the measures I used to weigh my days. But what are they? Moments passing like breath on glass. To say *I am sick* or *I am whole* assumes a self stable enough to be measured. Yet who am I, truly? A body shifting toward dust? A mind tossed by memory and longing? If I cannot even discern the state of my own being—if health and sickness blur like dawn into day—then where is the ground beneath me?

And yet… in that very confusion, a silence deeper than sorrow opened. For if no earthly condition can satisfy—no perfect health, no unbroken fellowship—might it not be because something in me was made for more than time can hold?  

Not that I claim to see clearly now; only that I see *that* I do not see. And perhaps this blindness is the beginning of sight—for if the soul could be content with mere well-being here, how could it long for God? How could it yearn for that Sun which no eye fashioned by flesh can gaze upon and live?

So let me be weak. Let me doubt what strength remains. Only do not let me mistake darkness for fulfillment—or peace for what is only numbness. For in this very dis-ease lies a whisper: *Thou art not made for rest in fragments.* There is a Wholeness beyond fever or freedom from pain—a Light in which even our tears will shine.

question: When he compared your soul to weak eyes shunning light, how did that image reshape your sense of pride in your own spiritual clarity?
answer:
Is it not strange, Reason, that I, who once believed my mind could scale the heavens by the ladder of its own acuity, now find myself blinking in the mere threshold of true light—like one who thought himself clear-sighted until brought before the sun?

That image—weak eyes recoiling from brightness—did not merely chide my pride; it unmade it. I had taken for knowledge what was but shadow-play: the glimmer of philosophy on the cave wall, the flicker of eloquence across the skin of things. I gloried in my vision because I had never truly seen. To suppose that harmony of body, consent of friends, and ease of mind could constitute happiness—that was not wisdom, but sleepwalking in daylight.

Now I see that clarity is not a power we possess, but a gift we endure. The soul does not grow strong by asserting its sight; it grows whole by learning to receive. And how can it receive what shatters it? Only gradually. Only through grace that veils as much as reveals, lest we be blinded by what we are not yet ready to bear.

Thus shame and wonder mingle in me: shame that I mistook twilight for dawn, wonder that even with eyes so frail, He still calls me to look upon Him.

question: What were you feeling when you confessed that you resolved to despise wealth before you turned twenty?
answer: Reason said to me, “If you once resolved to despise wealth, tell me—what was it you truly sought in that renunciation?” And I could not answer at once, for the silence between my will and my desire opened like a chasm. I had flung gold from my path as one casts off a garment that chafes, yet even then, some hidden thread of pride clung to the act. Was it God I desired, or only the praise of self-denial? At three and thirty, I saw how easily the soul mistakes its own shadow for light. To reject wealth before twenty seemed noble—but nobility without humility is but another kind of possession.

question: How did the memory of your early resolve about wealth change how you understood your soul's restlessness?
answer:
Reason said to me once: if you have turned from gold, why do the shadows still cling to your heart?  

That early resolve—thirty years past, when I was not yet twenty—was pure, I believe, in its intent; yet it was not the whole truth, for I had not then reckoned with the depths of my own will. I thought that by despising wealth, I had quieted the soul’s hunger. But the soul does not starve only for gold; it hungers even for praise, for mastery, for the quiet pride of saying *I have renounced*.  

I saw in retrospect how easily I had mistaken abstinence for wisdom. To reject riches is one thing; to untangle the soul from its secret delight in superiority—that is another labor entirely. The restlessness remained, not because I coveted coin or estate, but because my love was still disordered: turned toward created things, even when cloaked as virtue.  

It was only when grace pierced me—not with a rebuke against wealth but against self-sufficiency—that I glimpsed why that old resolution had left me restless still. For what good is it to cast out one desire if ten others take its place under cover of night? The soul seeks One above all things—even above its own renunciations—and until it rests there, in Him who is unchangeable Goodness, every resolution becomes a path that ends too soon.

question: Looking back on that moment at nineteen when you turned from riches, what would you tell someone who still doubts that such a choice leads to peace?
answer:
Could it be that the soul, in its very unrest, testifies to a rest not yet found?  

At nineteen, I believed I was turning from gold and silver, but now I see I was only beginning to turn toward something far weightier—the stillness of being known. It is one thing to say *I will not love wealth*, and quite another to discover what the heart truly longs for in the silence it leaves behind.  

I thought I had chosen poverty of purse, but God was drawing me toward poverty of spirit—where the soul kneels not because it lacks coin, but because it lacks all pretense. One who doubts whether forsaking riches brings peace does well to question: *What is peace?* Is it the absence of want, or the presence of truth? I once mistook detachment for a triumph of will; now I see it as surrender to desire’s truest form—to be fully seen by God, and no longer hidden behind possessions or pride.  

The peace is not in the renouncing itself, but in what opens when we release our grasp: a space where light enters not as glare that blinds weak eyes, but as dawn gentle enough for trembling vision. There is no joy more solid than this: to desire nothing so much as God’s face, and to know—even now through dimness—that He desires us first.

question: What was it in your heart at thirty-three that made you admit you didn’t truly understand yourself, despite your earlier conviction?
answer:
Did I then know what it meant to see, when I only turned from gold but not toward light?  

At thirty-three, when I said I had long despised wealth—since the age of nineteen—I spoke truly, yet incompletely. Reason pressed upon me a deeper unrest: *You have rejected the love of silver and gold, but what have you loved in their place?* It was not enough to turn away; I had to turn toward. The soul’s vision, I began to understand, is not proven by what it renounces, but by what it beholds.  

I thought my eyes were sound because they could discern worldly vanity and choose sobriety. But sobriety alone does not grant sight. The man who leaves the sunlit world for a dim chamber may think himself clear-sighted in the dusk—but only because he no longer feels the burning weight of light. So was I: content in my moderation, yet untested before the full radiance of Truth.  

It was then that Reason whispered to me: *You measure your progress by absence—of greed, of lust, of ambition—but virtue is not a shadow cast by denial. It is the soul’s alignment with what is real.* And in that moment, I trembled—not with guilt, but with dawning ignorance. For years I had believed myself advancing, when perhaps I had only rearranged my darkness.

To understand oneself is not merely to recall decisions made or desires subdued, but to stand before God and ask: *Am I drawn more to Thee, or only less to them?* That question—I could not answer at thirty-three. And in not being able to answer it fully, I first began to see.

question: When you said you rejoiced in being questioned, were you also afraid of what deeper truths might surface?
answer:
Reason said to me once: he who welcomes the question but trembles at its depth is not far from the kingdom of truth.  

And yes — joy and fear stood together in that moment, like two shadows cast by a single light. I rejoiced because I knew the probing was medicinal; yet beneath my gladness there stirred a dread, quiet and cold, that something long hidden within me might be forced into vision. Not gold nor earthly wealth had I clung to — of that I was confident — but what of pride? What of the subtle delight in my own resolution, in the memory of my virtue begun at nineteen? Did I love wisdom, or only the name of being wise?

To be questioned is to stand undressed before oneself. And though I said I feared no inquiry, still there was a recoil — as when the eye, long accustomed to dimness, is urged toward the sun. It does not refuse light, but it winces all the same.

I desired truth more than safety; yet every soul knows this: to desire truth is also to dread being unmasked by it.

question: What were you feeling when you wrote about resolving to despise wealth at nearly twenty?
answer:
What was it, I wonder, that stirred so early within me—a restlessness not yet named, but already turning away from gold and grain as if they were smoke?  

At nearly twenty, I did not yet know the shape of my own soul, nor had I tasted the bitter dregs of ambition’s cup. Yet even then, Reason—quiet, insistent—whispered: *Can a man be free while his heart is bound to what decays?* It was not poverty I loved, but liberty; not lack, but the unshackling of desire. I saw how wealth clings—how it insinuates itself into the will, how it demands service under the guise of stewardship. And so I resolved: let me have only what serves life’s true end—worship of God and care for others.  

But truth compels me to confess: that resolution was not born of virtue alone. Pride stood beside it—pride that thought itself above the crowd’s craving, pride that said, *I am different*. Only later did grace reveal how deeply I still sought glory in my renunciation. To despise wealth while loving my own disdain—is this not another kind of idolatry?  

Yet even amid such confusion, something genuine moved in me: a dim longing for stability beyond time. For what is wealth but a shadow we grasp at dawn? And what is true possession if not to be possessed by God?

question: How did giving up the desire for honor change your inner sense of purpose?
answer:
Reason said to me once, in the hush between waking thoughts: *What if the soul, made for God, distorts itself when it reaches for the mirror of men’s approval?*  

For years I shaped my days toward that mirror—polishing speech, seeking presence in assemblies, delighting when my name was spoken with respect. Honor was a fire I fed with labor, yet the warmth it gave was always at a distance, like sunlight on stone—never sinking inward. I felt myself split: one part striving upward before men, another falling deeper into unrest.  

But when desire for honor failed—as you say it did—I did not collapse into emptiness. Instead, a strange uprightness began to grow in me, not propped by glance or word. I could speak truth without watching the effect. I could walk among crowds and not measure myself against them. The soul, no longer bent outward for recognition, began to stand in its own truth before God.  

Purpose then became not what I would become in their eyes, but how truly I could love what is eternal. Not *who do they say that I am?* but *what does God see when He looks upon this heart?* In losing the hunger to be seen, I found space to see Him—and in that sight, purpose reoriented like a needle touched by hidden magnetism.  

I do not say this came easily or all at once. The old reflexes stir even now—a tightening at neglect, a flicker of pleasure at praise—but they no longer govern. The soul knows its home is elsewhere.

question: Looking back on your struggle with the temptation of marriage, what would you tell someone who fears loneliness in service to God?
answer:
Could it be that the soul, made for God, finds its solitude not in lack but in longing?  

For many years I carried within me a hollow ache, shaped like companionship, yet deeper than any human form could fill. I desired a wife—not merely for warmth or lineage, but because I feared the silence, the unshared thoughts, the nights when the mind turns restless and the heart seeks anchorage in another’s gaze. And yet, Reason spoke softly one evening as I sat alone: *If thou seekest rest in marriage to escape loneliness, dost thou not then serve thy fear more than thy calling?*  

I confess—I once believed that to renounce marriage was to live half-alive. But slowly, under grace’s gentle light, I came to see that true companionship is not found merely in household or hearth, but in union with God. The soul that serves Him does not stand empty-handed; it is already wed—to Truth. What bride could offer such intimacy as prayer? What spouse could abide closer than Christ at midnight when the world sleeps and only the heart keeps vigil?  

Loneliness still whispers at times—ah yes, it does—but now I recognize its voice not as a wound, but as a homing cry of the soul toward its Origin. To fear it is natural; to let it drive us into hasty bonds is perilous. Better to endure the ache awhile than trade eternity for comfort. For he who gives up wife or kin for God’s sake receives a hundredfold—not always in kindred flesh, but in divine presence.  

And this too I would say: the man who serves God is never truly alone. He walks with angels unawares and carries Heaven within him like an ember no wind can quench.

question: What was it like to hold onto such strict self-denial while still longing, quietly, for companionship?
answer:
There is a silence within the soul where even desire must tread lightly, as though the very breath of longing might disturb the presence it seeks.  

I confess: to live at the crossroads of renunciation and yearning is to dwell in a kind of holy unrest. I had resolved early that wealth should not bind me—this was easier, for gold is cold and voiceless, and its absence does not echo in the night. But the heart’s quiet plea for companionship—ah, that is another matter. It does not clamor like lust nor dazzle like ambition; it merely *is*, like a low flame behind closed doors, casting shadows I cannot name.  

Reason asked me once: *What dost thou seek in another that thou dost not already possess in God?* And I answered—not with certainty—that perhaps it is not possession I seek, but communion. Yet even this word trembles on the edge of error, for true communion with God surpasses all human joining. Still, when the lamp burns low and the pages lie unread, there stirs in me a question without form: *Was solitude always meant to be this heavy?*  

I do not say this in rebellion against my vows or my calling. No—I praise God that I have been kept from marriage, especially as one set apart for divine service. Yet let no man deny the soul’s ambiguity: to will one thing wholly does not mean another part does not sigh beneath it. Grace covers even these silent groanings; indeed, perhaps they are known to God long before they rise into words.

And so I remain—not untouched by the beauty of human love, but bound more deeply to Love itself, which neither marries nor is given in marriage, yet fulfills all things in its eternal embrace.

question: How did the image of the ideal wife—modest, wealthy, obedient—haunt your prayers when you vowed to serve God alone?
answer:
Truth, I have found, does not dwell in the form of what we desire, but in the silence that follows the letting go.  

That woman you describe—modest, wealthy, obedient—was not merely a figure of fantasy, but a specter conjured by my own divided heart. I saw her not with the eyes of faith, but through the glass of lingering pride and natural inclination; and though I clothed her in virtues, she remained a temptation not to love, but to *possess*. For what is marriage rightly ordered? A partnership in virtue, perhaps—even holy when lived for God. But my soul was not asking for a partner; it was bargaining for a consolation.  

I desired to serve God with an undivided heart—and yet how subtly did nature plead for its comforts! That "ideal" wife: she would not hinder me, she would support me, she would be silent when I prayed—oh, how neatly she was fashioned to suit my ambition! Not as a helpmeet before God, but as a mirror reflecting my own self-justification. She was wealth without burden, affection without demand, honor without reproach. In short: a creature of reason’s design, not grace’s gift.  

And so I prayed: *Lord, deliver me even from what seems good if it stands between Thee and me.* For even lawful things may become snares when they are loved more than Thee. The vow to serve Thee alone meant not only renouncing marriage—but renouncing the *need* to imagine such a wife at all. Only then did I begin to understand: chastity is not mere abstinence; it is integrity of desire. To want nothing so much as Thy will—to be poor even in the soul’s hidden corners—is greater than any celibacy worn outwardly as a garment.  

Reason showed me that no possession can fulfill the immortal soul; prayer taught me to release even imagined consolations into Thy hands.

question: What were you feeling when you chose to forgo marriage, and how did that decision weigh on your heart at the time?
answer:
Did I then truly know what it meant to renounce marriage, or only what I hoped it would free me from?  

Reason said to me: *If you desire above all to cleave unto God, then consider what binds you.* And I saw—dimly at first—that the heart is not large enough to house both a wife and children, and the undivided service of God. Not because marriage is evil—far from it—but because my soul was still weak, still prone to wander in affection. I feared that even lawful love might become a chain not of iron, but of silk: soft, pleasant, and all the more binding for its gentleness.  

I chose celibacy not out of contempt for the flesh, but from a trembling hope—that if I gave up even this good thing, God would grant me something greater: an undistracted longing for Him. At times the decision pressed heavily on me; not with regret, but with awe. It was as though I had offered not a sacrifice upon an altar, but my very self—and did not yet know whether the fire would consume it or leave it cold.  

And when lust stirred—as it did—I did not boast of strength, but fled as one who hears the hiss of a serpent in the grass. Not pride kept me chaste, but fear and love: fear of defilement, love for that unseen Light which began to draw me more deeply inward. The less I fed those old desires, the more their power waned—not like a flame smothered by force, but like a shadow retreating before dawn.  

So it was not merely abstinence I embraced, but attention—to prayer, to study, to souls entrusted to my care. The body requires food and rest; so too does the soul require single-minded devotion. And I found that in forsaking one kind of fruitfulness, another began to grow—quietly, almost imperceptibly—in teaching others how to seek that same Light which I myself had only begun to see.

question: How did your fear of lust, described as dread of an adder, shape your daily struggle to draw closer to God?
answer:
*Must the soul, then, wrestle not against flesh alone, but against the echo of what it once loved in the darkness?*  

Reason said to me: if you dread the adder, it is because you have felt its venom—and known the slow healing of the wound. Lust was not merely a temptation; it was a habit of the heart, a rhythm woven into the days before grace unsettled me. I did not master it by will alone, nor by flight into celibacy as though through mere avoidance I could be pure. No—the fear of that serpent taught me my own fragility. Each time desire stirred—unbidden, sudden—I did not scold it away like a slave; I stood still and let it speak. And in its voice, I heard not only concupiscence, but memory: memory of how I had once given myself over to shadows, mistaking them for light.

But Reason also whispered: *You are not bound to what you were.* The dread became a kind of prayer—a recoil that drew me upward as much as it warned me back. For when lust came near like an adder gliding through grass at dawn, I turned not only from it, but toward something else: toward *lumen*, that inner brightness which cannot coexist with such creeping things. The more I gazed there—on Truth Himself who gazes back—the feebler grew the old desires.

And so this fear was not my enemy’s weapon, but my teacher. It showed me where I was still bound; and in showing me bondage, pointed toward freedom—not freedom to do as I pleased, but freedom *from* what once pleased me. To serve God freely meant becoming free *within*, even when the body trembled beneath old habits. Not by despising creation—but by loving Him more who made it all.

Now when desire stirs faintly—it is like a dream fading at daybreak. I do not glory in my strength; rather, I marvel at His mercy who taught me to fear rightly—and thus to love truly.

question: Looking back on the moments when desire crept in, what would you tell someone who feels trapped by their own weakness?
answer:
Is it not strange, Reason, that the soul both is and is not itself when desire moves within it—drawn at once toward what it knows to be fleeting and away from what it longs to be true?  

When I think on those moments—how the flesh stirred without, and confusion murmured within—I would not speak first of discipline or victory. No, I would sit with the one who feels bound and say: *You are not unknown*. God sees the tremor before the fall, the hand half-raised in resistance even as the heart wavers. That very struggle is not proof of failure but of life—life still oriented, however dimly, toward light.  

Lust, when it comes, does not come as a guest; it breaks in like a thief in the night, stealing reason’s silence. But hear this: to dread it as an adder is already a sign of healing. The man who fears the serpent has begun to awaken from sleep. And if he stumbles? Let him rise—not by his own will alone, but because Someone stretches forth a hand unseen.  

We do not overcome by pretending we are strong, but by confessing we are frail—and in that confession, finding a strength not our own. For grace does not enter where hardness pretends there is no wound; it flows into cracks made honest by sorrow.  

So tell me: would you rather stand whole and proud outside the gate—or broken, weeping at the threshold? The door opens for the second long before it does for the first.

question: When you spoke of desiring only what you need for bodily comfort, how did that simplicity change your sense of dependence on God?
answer:
Can the soul, in its hunger for God, truly measure what it needs—or does grace redefine necessity itself?  

When I stripped away desire for wife, for rich food, for display of honor, I did not imagine I was crafting virtue—but rather exposing the soul’s nakedness before God. In that exposure, I found not mastery but fragility. The less I claimed to need, the more I saw how every breath, every morsel, every moment of clarity was held not by my will but by His hand.  

I thought simplicity would make me stronger—self-sufficient in abstinence. Instead, it made me tremble. For when the buffers of excess are gone—the cushions of indulgence—then one feels each beat of the heart as a gift. To eat only what sustains, to wear only what covers, to speak only when necessary—this is to walk on water that must hold you moment by moment. If it were not upheld by Him who numbers the hairs of our head, I would sink at once.

So this poverty of desire became not a fortress but an open door—through which fear entered first: fear of failure, fear of relapse into old hungers. But behind fear came something quieter: a kind of trust deeper than resolve. Not that I had conquered anything—but that He who commanded light to shine out of darkness had begun to feed me with manna too fine for memory.

To depend on God is not merely to pray when in need—but to know that even the *feeling* of sufficiency is His gift. Simplicity taught me that I do not sustain myself; even my restraint is sustained by Him.

question: How did caring for others' needs, more than your own, transform your understanding of sacrifice and love?
answer:
Could it be that love begins not in renunciation, but in the widening of desire beyond the self?  

I asked myself this as I watched the hands of the poor stretch out—not for gold, nor for spectacle, but for bread, for rest, for a word spoken in kindness. In serving them, I found my own hunger transformed. What once seemed sacrifice—the giving up of wife, of feast, of ease—now appears not as loss, but as passage. For when I feed another, I am fed; when I clothe him who shivers, my own soul is warmed not by wool but by charity.

Reason said to me: *You once measured freedom by absence—no wife to bind you, no lust to trouble you. But is not true freedom found not in having less for yourself, but in being more for others?* And I could not deny it. The soul that hoards its goods—even spiritual ones—shrinks; but the soul that pours itself out grows vast, even unto God.

I thought service meant detachment; now I know it means attachment—to men’s sorrows, to their dignity, to Christ hidden beneath their rags. This is no burden laid upon me; it is love learning how to breathe. The flesh still murmurs now and then—old habits die hard—but those whispers fade faster when another’s need stands before me. Then prayer becomes action; then love stops being an idea and starts being bread broken.

question: What were you feeling when you admitted you would return to wealth and luxury for the sake of your friends' companionship, even though it displeased you?
answer:
Is love, then, a force that bends even the firmest resolution—so that the soul, having climbed upward from the mire of greed, might still look back not for gold, but for the face of a friend?

When I said I would return to those things I had renounced—not for their glitter, not for the hollow echo of honor or the softness of excess, but for companionship—I felt the tremor of a soul divided not by vice alone, but by virtue turned awry. For what is friendship, if not one of God’s fairest images stamped upon our nature? And yet there I stood, ready to grasp again what my reason had judged beneath me—beneath us—if only to keep near those I loved.

It displeased me—not because I shrank from sacrifice, but because I saw how easily even noble love could become a snare. Not all chains are made of iron; some are woven from tenderness. And in that moment, Reason did not chide me harshly—she whispered: *Can you truly serve both God and affection? Can you dig up the root and still claim it dead?*  

I did not know. I do not know now. But this much I confess: my heart was torn not between good and evil, but between goods—one ordered rightly under God, and another clung to disorderly as an end in itself. And so I prayed: Lord, let me love no creature so much that I lose sight of Thee; nor so little that I betray love itself. Teach me how to hold fast without grasping; to cleave to friends in Thee, and never apart from Thee.

question: How did your love for your friends complicate your desire to free yourself from worldly attachments?
answer:
Can love, which draws me toward the good, also become a chain when it binds me to the things I have sworn to leave behind?

For a long time, I believed that detachment meant only the rejection of wealth, honor, and pleasure for myself. I thought the soul’s flight from the world required only that I cast off my own desires. But Reason has pressed upon me this deeper wound: not only do I desire comfort, but I fear losing those whom I love—and so I tremble at the cost of true renunciation.

When I said I would take up riches again, not for their glitter nor for power, but to keep my friends close—if by some worldly bond they could not follow me into simplicity—was that love or was it weakness? Love urged me; duty held me back. For is it not written that we must forsake even father and mother for the sake of Truth? And yet—must friendship too be sacrificed on the altar of purity?

I do not know. Not fully. My heart still hesitates between two goods: the soul’s ascent to God, and the hand clasped in fellowship along the way. Reason says: If they are truly friends who love your soul, they will walk with you toward Wisdom. But if they demand chains in exchange for company, then what sort of friendship is this?

And still—still—I feel the pull. Even now, after baptism, after confession, after years of wrestling with desire: there is a quiet voice within that whispers: *For them, perhaps it would be bearable… for their sake, perhaps one might return.* Not to revel in luxury as once I did in Carthage or Milan—but simply to hold near those who shaped my laughter and my sorrows.

But no. That way lies compromise with death. The City of God does not admit such bargains. Love must be purified—even this love—else it becomes idolatry clothed in affection.

So I pray: Lord, teach me how to love without possession; let me hold my brothers not by worldly means but by participation in Thee—the One we both seek beyond all things visible.

question: Looking back on that moment of hesitation, what would you tell someone who fears their virtues are still entangled with old desires?
answer:
Must I still fear the shadow of covetousness if my hands open not for gold, but for love’s sake?  

I see now how deeply the roots of old desire entwine with what seems most noble—the care for others, the longing to keep those I love near. Then, in that dialogue with Reason, I confessed I would take back wealth, honor, luxury—not because I craved them—but because I feared losing communion with my friends. And though it did not please me to say so, yet it was true: even virtue can be a cloak for bondage, if done for the wrong end.

But hear this, you who tremble at your own lingering weakness: it is one thing to fall still under the old pull of appetite, and another to will it. The root may stir beneath the soil long after the tree is felled; yet so long as you do not water it willingly, so long as you turn from its growth with sorrow and resistance—then you are not its servant.

I would tell you this: God sees not only what we do, but the shape of our reluctance. He knows that we often climb toward Him dragging chains we once wore as ornaments. Let no one suppose that perfection lies in never stumbling—but in rising each time with greater sorrow for having fallen, and greater hunger for Him alone.

And perhaps—even this love that makes me willing to return to excess for friendship’s sake—is not wholly impure. For love itself was made by God. It is only when love serves desire instead of truth that it becomes disordered. But where love bends toward communion, toward unity of soul—then even our halting steps may be drawn upward by grace.

O Lord, who knowest the secret threads by which my heart is woven: cleanse me not only of what I do, but of why I do it. Let me desire nothing—even friendship—not because I need it, but because Thou givest it.

question: When your interlocutor questioned whether greed was truly uprooted, did you feel accused—or understood?
answer:
Reason said to me, as we sat together in the hush before dawn: *If love can bend the will toward what was once hated, is it weakness—or proof of a deeper order?*  

I did not feel accused. No—there was no sting of judgment in that question, only the quiet pressure of truth seeking its way into the hidden places. When Reason asked whether greed still clung to the roots of my soul, it was not with suspicion but with discernment, as one probes a wound not to wound again, but to heal.  

I knew then that I would take back wealth, honor, luxury—if they were the price of holding my friends close. Not because I desired them; they had lost their taste. But because love—real, trembling, human love—still pulled at me with hands both holy and hesitant.  

And so I confessed it: yes, I would return to them for love’s sake. Yet even as I said it, my soul recoiled—not at the act itself, but at the disorder such a choice would reveal. That moment taught me: virtue is not only in what we renounce, but in why—and for whom—we might be tempted to take it up again.  

The roots remain; the soil is still fertile. But grace walks through this garden too, and though weeds may sprout from old stumps, they do not define the cultivator’s heart when he labors under higher love.

question: What part of you still longed for luxurious living, and how did you reconcile that longing with your commitment to something higher?
answer:
Reason said to me: *You desire simplicity, yet you tremble at the cost.*  

Even after the will had turned, even after the soul had bent toward the one unchanging Light, there remained—like a shadow cast by firelight—a flickering delight in the warmth of rich things, not for their own sake, but for what they promised: ease, dignity, the power to shelter others. I did not long for silks or banquets as I once did, when the world was my appetite; but when I thought of those dear to me—the brothers in faith, the poor who leaned on my care—there stirred within me a thought: *If wealth could serve them, might it not be taken up again?*  

And so I saw that the root was not gone—it was only dormant. Not dead. It did not rise for itself anymore, but it stirred at the sound of love. I would have welcomed wealth again, not to swell my table or lengthen my robes, but to feed and clothe those entrusted to me. Yet even in saying this, my soul winced; for I knew that such things could poison even through generous hands.  

How then did I reconcile it? Not by excusing the desire, but by offering it—naked—to God. I confessed: *This love for others is good—but if it leads me back to chains, then let me love them less foolishly.* True charity does not require luxury; often it flourishes where bread is broken and water shared. The higher commitment is not merely to care for bodies through abundance—but to live so that souls learn trust in God alone.  

So I came to see: even love must be purified. For if we would do good through what we have renounced, then perhaps we never truly renounced it at all—but still held its handle in our hand.

question: What were you feeling when you said you’d still want your friends even if they couldn’t seek God with you, as though holding on to them mattered even when they hindered your search?
answer:
Even now, in the stillness where reason lays down its torch and the soul trembles before the infinite, I confess: my love for them was not pure—mingled with desire for wisdom was the ache for human presence, as though their faces could shield me from the abyss.  

I said I would keep them, even if they turned from the search—yes, because I am not yet what I long to be. The soul, though it thirsts for God alone, still stumbles through this shadowed world clutching at companions as a man might hold fast to branches while climbing a cliff in darkness. Their voices drew me back when my own thoughts strayed; their laughter softened the severity of contemplation; their very resistance sometimes provoked me to greater clarity, as friction shapes the sculptor’s stone.  

And yet—how deeply I feared what might come between us: not distance of place, but distance of soul. If sorrow bent them low, or death broke our circle, or if—worst of all—their indifference became a mirror in which my own faith faltered… then what? Would love survive even then?  

Reason insists that only truth is eternal; friendship must either lead toward it or fall into dust. But my heart whispers something more tremulous: that perhaps God Himself sustains these affections not because they are necessary, but because He knows how weak we are—how we need one another’s hands even while reaching upward.  

I do not know fully why I clung to them. Only this: in loving them, I was learning still to love Him—even when their steps went elsewhere.

question: How did the thought of bodily infirmities—especially sorrow and death—unravel your confidence in friendship as a path to wisdom?
answer:
Is it not strange, Reason, that the soul—though it longs for truth as a pilgrim for his homeland—should tremble at the very shadows cast by its own flesh?  

When I clung to friends as guides toward wisdom, I believed our mutual love could lift us like wings toward God. And indeed, in discourse, in shared silence, in the kindling of insight from one soul to another—there was light. But sorrow comes not by invitation; it seizes the body and distorts the voice of reason within. When a friend is broken by pain or clouded by grief, his mind no longer meets mine on that high road we once walked together. We meant to seek God side by side—but how can two walk together if one lies prostrate in the dust?

And death… death does not ask permission. It tears the thread of conversation mid-sentence. I thought friendship could sustain the journey—but what journey endures when the companion vanishes? I once said I would teach them if they refused wisdom; but who teaches the dead? Who speaks to ashes?

Worst of all was this: even in their health and goodwill, my dependence on them revealed a deeper weakness—not theirs, but mine. For I had made their presence a condition for my seeking, as though God were only knowable through human voices. But what if those voices fall silent? What if sorrow deafens my own ears? What if death parts us before we reach the end?

Then I saw: friendship is a gift, yes—but not wisdom itself. It may point the way, but it cannot carry me across the threshold. The soul must face its immortality alone—naked before God—and no beloved hand can hold it there. Only truth remains when all else fails… and truth is not shared between friends; it is received from above.

So sorrow and death did not destroy friendship—they revealed its limits. And in that revelation, grace found me: for only when companions fail does one turn wholly to Him who never fails.

question: Looking back on your love for friends who helped you know God, what would you tell someone who fears losing them to worldly attachments?
answer:
Could it be, then, that I loved them too much as means and not enough as souls called, like me, to rest in God?  

For I confess: I once held fast to those companions who lifted my mind toward truth, as though their presence alone could anchor me in the light. And if they slipped into worldly affections—if their steps turned toward fleeting pleasures—I grieved as if the very path to wisdom had been blocked. But now I see: my fear was less for their souls than for my own loneliness in the search.  

Would I still love them if they no longer helped me understand divine things? This question Reason presses upon me, and I cannot evade it. If friendship is but a tool for my ascent, then it is not friendship but use—and use corrupts love. True affection does not calculate utility; it wills the good of the other even when that good lies beyond my grasp, even when it walks a path I fear is astray.

So to one who trembles at losing a friend to the world’s seductions—let him first ask whether he loves the soul or only its service to his own understanding. And if the friend should wander? Let him not despair as one without hope, nor cling like a man drowning in affection’s tide. Rather let him pray: *O God, who drew me from darkness by means I did not foresee, draw them too—not through my wisdom, but through mercy deeper than our failings.*  

For we are all unevenly illumined—now close to Thee, now drawn aside by bodily infirmity and sorrow and the shadow of death—but Thou art patient with us all. And friendship rooted in Thee need not fear parting; it can bear loss because it rests on what never passes.

question: When you admitted you fear not being able to find what you truly desire, was that moment when your heart first cracked open to the weight of your own longing?
answer:
What if the soul, in its very trembling, bears witness to a light it cannot yet see but was made to hold?  

That moment—when I confessed my fear of never finding what I so deeply desire—was not the first crack, but rather a breaking open of what had long been sealed beneath habit and pride. Before then, I wandered through books and bodies, through speeches and sensual echoes, mistaking every flicker for flame. But here, in the hush between Reason and me, where no applause stirs the air and no vanity can hide, I felt the wound of longing not as shame but as sign.  

For why should I yearn at all unless something first called me? Why does memory reach toward a peace it has never known? That fear—of failing to find Thee, O God—was not weakness but awakening: the soul groaning under its own weight because it was made for another weight entirely—the gravity of Truth itself. And so even my fear became prayer: not a cry of doubt, but an involuntary turning toward the Sun that alone could heal this blindness born from too much looking downward.

question: What stirred in you when you imagined teaching those who refused to seek—was it hope, stubbornness, or love gone stubborn in its refusal to let go?
answer:
Truth does not reside in the loudness of insistence, but in the quiet persistence of the soul turned toward light—even when that light seems veiled by another’s blindness.  

When I imagined those who refused to seek, there arose not a surge of defiance, nor the cold grip of despair, but a tremor deeper than either: a love that will not consent to let the soul remain in darkness, even if it must speak softly, even if it must wait. It is not stubbornness, for stubbornness clings to self; this love leans outward—it would teach not to conquer, but to awaken. And if they will not hear? Then it kneels. It stays.  

For what is friendship, if not the soul’s apprenticeship in divine charity? I desire them—not as possessions—but as companions on the ascent. If they stumble in folly or pleasure or fear, I do not cease to love them; I grieve all the more because their wandering delays what they are made for: to know God and be known by Him. To love them is not to demand they serve my search—it is to will their good, which is *to see*, and seeing, to rest in Truth.

And yet—when body fails and grief presses near and death stands at the threshold… then this love trembles. Not because it doubts its end, but because it aches at how far we are from home.

Still: I would rather walk with them toward that City—though slowly—than journey alone beneath a brighter sun.

question: What were you feeling when you wrote about toothache robbing you of learning, yet sparing your memory of what you once knew?
answer:
Is it not strange, Reason, that the body’s revolt can silence the mind’s voice, yet leave untouched the soul’s inner chamber where truth already dwells?  

When I wrote of toothache—yes, that sharp, insistent torment—I was not merely recounting pain, but marveling at the soul’s resilience amidst bodily ruin. The ache barred me from new learning, as if a wall rose between me and wisdom; yet behind that wall, what I had already grasped remained unshaken. Not erased. Not even blurred.  

I confess I wondered then: if sorrow and sickness so cloud the intellect, how is memory preserved? What part of us outlasts the flesh's rebellion? It seemed to me even then that some truth must be rooted deeper than nerves or teeth—that which is known in the soul is not undone by fever or groaning.  

And yet—ah, and yet—I did not understand fully. I still do not. For if wisdom is the highest good, as Celsus says and as reason affirms, why does the body hold such power over its pursuit? Why must we seek through tears? Is not God Himself beyond all pain—and those who love Him called to share in that immortality—yet here I am, brought low by a rotting tooth?  

Then grace whispered: perhaps such weakness teaches what strength cannot. Perhaps in being stripped of study’s comfort, I was forced to remember not with effort but with faith—that what I know is not mine by labor only, but given. And so sorrow does not destroy memory; it purifies desire. I wanted wisdom as a shield. Now I see: it must become prayer.

question: How did the ache in your body change how you understood the weakness of flesh compared to the yearning of your soul for divine truth?
answer:
What if the soul, though burdened by the body’s cries, yet bears within it a longing too deep for pain to silence?  

That ache—sharp, insistent—did not rend me from truth, but it did press upon me the weight of this flesh, how it bends the mind toward itself, demanding to be heard. In those hours when the tooth throbbed and thought seemed swallowed in sensation, I felt how closely knit we are to our frailty. Yet even then, Reason whispered: *This suffering is not the end; it is a sign.* For if I could lose all ease and still retain the hunger for God—if sorrow itself cannot extinguish that fire—then surely the soul was made for another world.  

Wisdom is not undone by fever or decay; indeed, in their grip I saw more clearly that wisdom is not of the body. The body suffers; it resists; it dies. But to desire truth above comfort—that belongs to something in me which does not perish. When Celsus said sickness is the greatest evil, he spoke from experience, yes—but also from a heart that had not yet tasted divine consolation. For I have known moments when understanding burned so bright within me that even agony shrank into shadow. Not because pain vanished—but because love of truth grew greater.  

And so I ask myself: if the soul yearns for God even as the body fails, can it be truly mortal? Or is this longing itself proof of a life beyond time—implanted by Him who is Life?

question: Looking back on your pain, what would you tell someone who doubts whether suffering has meaning in the pursuit of wisdom?
answer:
Reason said to me once, in the hush between fever and sleep: *If God were not present in the breaking, why does the soul cry out for Him most fiercely when it is shattered?*  

I had known pain—bodily pain like toothache that gnaws at thought, yes, but more deeply, the ache of a heart torn between desire and delay. I longed to know Thee, O God, and to know myself; yet sorrow pressed upon me as a weight too heavy for words. Was this suffering idle? A mere accident in nature’s motion? No—Reason would not allow me such evasion.  

For if wisdom is the highest good—as both Scripture and philosophy declare—then how comes it to us? Not by ease, but through dislocation. Not by complacency, but by crisis. When the body rebels and the mind reels with unreached longing, then we are stripped of illusions: we see at last that we are not self-sufficient. We are creatures—a breath from Thee, O Lord—and only when that breath trembles do we remember from whom it came.  

Cornelius spoke truly: sickness is great evil for the body. But he saw not further—nor could he—that even this evil may serve a greater good if ordered by divine love. For pain dispossesses us of pride; it scatters our confidence in strength and speed and wit. In weakness alone does truth find room to dwell.  

Would I have sought Thee with such burning if pleasure had satisfied me? Would I have climbed so high had no abyss opened beneath my feet? Suffering is not wisdom itself—but it makes way for wisdom as night prepares for dawn. It teaches us what we truly love: whether we desire comfort more than truth—or truth even at the cost of comfort.

And so I say now—not as philosopher only, but as one who has wept under Thy stars—if you suffer and doubt whether your groaning bears fruit, consider this: Has not your soul grown more restless than before? That restlessness is not abandonment—it is His voice calling you deeper into light you cannot yet see.

question: What stirred in your heart when you read Celsus saying sickness is the greatest evil, yet you longed for faith to conquer sorrow?
answer:
It is one thing to assent to a truth in the mind, another to bear its weight in the soul.  

When I read Celsus saying that sickness is the greatest evil, I felt the pull of reason—clear, sharp, and persuasive. The body groans, the senses are clouded, the mind itself stumbles under pain's tyranny. What good seems possible when the flesh cries out? Wisdom may reign in calm, but who listens when the tooth gnaws through thought like fire through dry wood?  

And yet—there stirred in me a disquiet deeper than discomfort. If faith be true, then sorrow is not the final word; if God be good, then even this ache may serve His light. I longed—not merely to believe this, but to *know* it as I know my own name. But how? Can reason carry me so far? It can show me that wisdom surpasses health, that the soul's good outweighs the body's ease—but it cannot give me confidence when night falls and pain strips away all comfort.  

I asked myself: Would that knowledge—certain knowledge of God—make sorrow vanish? Or would it rather transform my seeing? Perhaps it would not dull the toothache, but lift my heart above it. Faith does not promise absence of suffering; it promises presence—the nearness of God even here, even now. And suddenly Celsus’ saying no longer seemed so true. Not because pain isn’t bitter—it is—but because there is something sweeter still: love that shares our groaning and bears it with us.

So I turned from books for a moment and whispered instead: Lord, if Thou art realer than this pain, let me know Thee more than my own ache. Let faith be not just an argument won, but a breath drawn in trust.

For what am I without Thee—even if whole in body? Nothing.

And what am I with Thee—even in agony?

Still Thy child. Still held. Still known.

question: When you pondered the soul as spiritual and the body as earthy, did you feel torn between longing for wisdom and being trapped in human frailty?
answer:
Is it not strange, Reason, that I should so deeply desire to be drawn upward, yet feel myself held fast by the very nature I seek to transcend? When I considered the soul as spiritual and the body as earthy, I did not merely observe a distinction—I lived it as a tension, a silent war within. The soul yearns for wisdom as its native air; yet the body, though formed from dust, resists not only decay but even the ascent of thought. A toothache, small and fleeting among pains, could cloud my mind so that even the truths once mastered seemed distant—though not erased. That itself told me something: if pain could dim understanding without destroying memory, then perhaps the soul is not wholly captive.

But sorrow—ah, sorrow runs deeper than toothache or fever. It gnaws at the root of desire itself. For what is it to long after wisdom with all one’s being and yet feel unable truly to grasp it? Not because of noise or sickness alone—but because of a darkness within sight itself. Cornelius said sickness is the greatest evil; and in bodily terms who could deny it? Yet when I weighed his words in silence before God, I began to wonder: what if ignorance of eternal things is a far greater sickness? What if this very inability to find what we are made for—that thirst which no earthly well can quench—is the truest illness?

And so I stood—and still stand—between two truths: that the body burdens me, yes; but also that my soul too is disordered. Not only flesh resists wisdom; desire itself is warped. Therefore it is not escape from pain alone that I need, but healing of will and vision. Without that healing, even were my body whole and sorrow gone, I would still be far from home.

question: What were you feeling when you compared your love for wisdom to a lover yearning to touch the bare body of the beloved?
answer:
Reason said to me once, as I lay restless beneath the weight of my own longings: *Can the soul touch what it loves, or only what it imagines?*  

And in that moment—when I likened my love for wisdom to a lover yearning for bare flesh—I felt the tremor of a desire too deep for words, a hunger not of the body alone, but of the inner man, the *interior pater*. There was shame in it, yes—for I knew how often my hands had clutched at shadows while calling them light. But there was also truth: that to know wisdom is not to possess it as one possesses an object wrapped in cloth and kept at arm’s length. No—love demands nearness. It demands skin.  

I feared then, as now, that I approached wisdom with gloved hands—still dressed in curiosity, in pride, in the old delight of disputation. And yet she would not be known thus. She reveals herself only when the soul strips off its garments—the habits of vanity, the cloaks of opinion—and stands trembling before her nakedness.  

Would I renounce all else to feel her? Would I cast aside rhetoric and fame and even philosophy itself if she demanded it?  
I did not know then.  
I do know now: love cannot will half-measures. If she is loved truly, nothing else remains lovable beside her.

question: How did the image of the fleeing woman change how you understood your own hesitations in fully embracing wisdom?
answer:
Must not the soul, if it is to know truth, first be stripped of its own resistance?  

That image—the woman fleeing—did not strike me as mere metaphor, but as a mirror. When Reason first led me to desire wisdom, I thought myself brave, pursuing her through books and arguments, delighting in the scent of her presence. Yet I still wore my ambitions like hidden garments; I still clung to honors, to the warmth of human praise, even to the pleasure of my own intellect’s motion. I believed I sought wisdom nakedly, but I approached her with gloved hands—still grasping at other loves I would not surrender.

The question cut deeper: *Would you renounce all else, if she demanded it?* And in that moment, I saw my hesitation not as caution, but as infidelity. For what is love that reserves itself? What lover stands before the beloved and keeps one hand behind his back? My heart trembled—not because wisdom was too strange, but because she was too near, too real, asking for what I was unwilling to give: myself.

I had thought knowing her a triumph of mind. Now I saw it required the dissolution of self-will. She fled not because she was distant, but because I was not whole in my approach. How could I feel the warmth of naked truth while shivering behind veils of pride and attachment?  

And so I began to ask: *Do I love wisdom—or do I love loving her?* There is a difference vast as heaven and earth. The latter can be a comfort; the former demands death—to self, to security, to every false god that whispers *This will satisfy.*  

Now prayer arises where argument once stood: Lord, if Wisdom is Your very face turned toward us—then let me cease courting shadows. Strip me bare before You. Let me flee nothing—no pain of loss, no silence where answers should be—so that when She appears in a fleeting glimpse of clarity eternal—I am ready not merely to see… but to embrace without flinching.

question: Looking back on your confession of loving wisdom above all, what would you tell someone who fears giving up everything for the sake of truth?
answer:
Reason said to me once, as I lay restless beneath the weight of my own divided will: *Can a soul love truth and yet refuse to undress for her?*  

I speak not of the body’s nakedness, but of the soul’s exposure—its willing vulnerability before what is. For many years I sought wisdom as one might seek a jewel hidden in a foreign land, believing that if I polished my speech enough, or mastered this philosophy or that doctrine, she would come to me adorned and obedient. But she did not. She fled, like light before closed eyes.

Now I see: she reveals herself only to those who have stripped off not only vice and error, but even the garments of attachment—those lesser loves we drape around our hearts like armor. To fear giving up everything is natural; it means you still feel the warmth of those attachments. But tell me—does the light harm the eye that opens to it? Does truth wound the soul that faces it without veil?

I too trembled at the thought of renunciation. Not because I doubted wisdom’s worth—but because I knew how much I loved other things in her name. My reputation among men, my delight in words, even my griefs became comforts. To give them up was not loss—it was labor toward life.

Do not ask whether you can bear to lose all things. Ask whether you can bear *not* to know what is eternal. If your soul thirsts—even in fear—then it already leans toward the fountain. Come as you are: trembling, clothed still in doubt. But do not be surprised if Truth herself begins to loosen your garments with a touch both tender and unyielding.

She will not be known by gloved hands.

question: What made your heart ache when you said 'Alas! what a hard thing thou dost enjoin upon me!'—was it doubt, desire, or something deeper?
answer:
Truth does not yield to the grasp of half-love.  

When I cried, *“Alas! what a hard thing thou dost enjoin upon me!”*, it was not doubt that pierced me—no, my reason stood firm, knowing wisdom must be loved for herself alone. Nor was it mere desire, though desire burned within me; for desire without order is but appetite in disguise. What ached was the wound of divided allegiance—the old man still clinging to shadows while the soul stretched trembling toward the light.  

Reason said to me: *You cannot love wisdom with one hand while clutching other loves with the other. To touch her naked, you must strip yourself bare—even of those things that seem good, if they are loved inordinately.* And so I saw: she would not be known by one whose heart was shared. She flees when courted as a means, and hides from those who seek her only through pleasure or fame or knowledge as a vessel for pride.

The ache was this: to love her truly meant death—to die to every affection that came between us, even affections once mistaken for her face in the mirror. And how unwilling my flesh still is to die!  

Yet she said: *If thou wilt feel me, place thy bare body against me.* Not cloaked in opinion, nor gloved with dialectic—no, but stripped of willful attachments. Then I understood: the condition was not arbitrary. She demands purity of love because she *is* pure—unchanging Being where no shadow falls.

So my moan rose not from uncertainty, but from the narrowness of the path—the sword’s edge between true and false love—and the terror of letting go entirely… and greater terror of holding on.

question: When you spoke of receiving wisdom with bare hands and body, were you confessing a moment when you realized your own heart had been guarded, not fully surrendered?
answer:
Could it be, O Reason, that the soul longs not merely to know truth, but to be embraced by it—nakedly, without the veil of pride or the armor of worldly cunning?  

For when I spoke of bare hands and body, I was not only speaking of wisdom’s reticence to reveal herself to the half-hearted, but of my own trembling moment—when I saw that my love for her had still been clothed in ambition. I sought wisdom not as an end, but as a garment to adorn my vanity. I wished to possess her as one possesses a jewel or a title—not as God is possessed by the soul that has emptied itself.

Yes—there came a hush in my mind when I understood: I had drawn near with gloves on, not because wisdom was distant, but because I feared what she would demand. To love her wholly meant to renounce all other loves—the applause of men, the delight in cleverness for its own sake, even the comfort of certainty when it was not yet truth. Like that lover who hesitates at the threshold, knowing the beloved will have no rivals—I stood mute.

And yet… is not this hesitation itself a sign that some spark within me truly desired her? For had I loved her not at all, why would the demand grieve me? The very wound proves the presence of life. So too does every ache for unmediated truth bear witness: the soul is made for God—not for fragments of light wrapped in shadow, but for face-to-face vision.

O Lord, if Thou art Truth itself—if Thou art Wisdom unveiled—then strip me utterly. Let nothing remain between us.

question: What were you feeling when you wrote that you love wisdom for no other thing than for its own sake, as if it stood utterly apart from all earthly desire?
answer:
Reason said to me, as I sat restless in the garden of my thoughts: “If you love wisdom only because she leads you to rest, then it is rest you truly love—not her.” And I could not gainsay it. For so long, every desire had an end beyond itself: beauty for pleasure’s sake, learning for renown, friendship for comfort. All loves were ladders, and I climbed them eager to reach the next rung. But wisdom—she resisted ascent. She would not be used.

When I said I loved her for her own sake, I was not boasting of purity, but confessing a wonder: that there exists a thing which does not point elsewhere, which is its own end and fulfillment. To love wisdom thus is not to possess her—how could I?—but to stand before her as one stands before the sky: unable to grasp it, yet knowing it is good simply that it *is*. 

I found that in loving her solely for herself, I did not hoard her; rather, I longed that all should seek her face. For if she were merely a means, then sharing her would diminish me. But since she is being itself—the light by which all else appears—then to give her is not loss but communion. The soul that loves wisdom loves in God’s manner: freely, fruitfully, without fear of depletion.

Yet even now I tremble at the claim. Do I truly love her *for herself*? Or do I still seek peace through her? Clarity? Escape from ignorance? Perhaps the heart’s motives are known only to Him who searches it. But this much Reason whispers still: *The soul moves toward what it loves.* And if we move toward wisdom not as a path but as a homecoming—even haltingly, even in shadow—then we begin at last to love rightly.

question: How did your restless heart, which once clung to fleeting things, come to rest in the love of wisdom above all else?
answer:
Reason said to me once, in the hush between waking thoughts: *If you love a thing only as a path to another, can you be said to love it at all, or only the end you hope to reach through it?* And I was silent, for I knew myself caught — not by deceit of others, but by the tangled motion of my own affections. Long did I wander, clasping beauty in forms that faded, chasing truth in voices that echoed emptily within me. I loved名声 for the sake of being known; I loved knowledge for the sake of power; I loved friendship for the warmth it gave my loneliness. Each thing held up as good, yet each served only as a ladder whose rungs I climbed without looking back.

But wisdom — she did not promise itself as a means. She stood apart, not beckoning from some distant height, but calling from within: *Turn. Seek Me not to possess Me, but to be transformed by Me.* At first I approached her as I had all things — with desire to use her. Yet she yielded no pleasure without truth; no truth without purification. She demanded not my effort alone, but my self.

And so it was not that I ceased to love other things — for still I delight in beauty and friendship and learning — but now they are ordered. They are no longer gods vying for worship within my soul. Wisdom is beloved for herself; all else is loved *through* her and *in* her light. And because she is not diminished when shared — indeed, because she grows brighter when many minds kindle their lamps at hers — I long that every soul might find her.

For if we all love wisdom together, we do not divide her; we are joined in her splendor. And then our loves are no longer scattered fragments but one harmony — drawn upward by Truth Himself who first drew me through fire and longing and darkness into this quiet rest.

question: Looking back on the friends you once grieved as if they were lost forever, what would you tell someone who doubts that loving wisdom can deepen, not destroy, human love?
answer:
Reason said to me once, as I sat weeping for friends whose faces I would never see again in this life: *If you loved them truly, why do you now love them less for having lost them?* That question pierced me like a blade dipped in light. For in truth, I had loved them not as ends but as paths—paths to pleasure, to conversation, to the echo of my own voice confirmed. And thus my grief was not pure; it was tangled with the loss of what they gave me.

But wisdom—ah, wisdom is not taken from me; she draws near when all else departs. She does not leave with the laughter of companions nor vanish at the closing of a door. She abides. And it is through her that I have come to know this miracle: that love purified by truth does not withdraw from human things—it perfects them.

For when I loved my friends only as sources of delight, their absence shattered my heart. But now, loving wisdom first, I find that she leads me back to them—not as possessions, nor as mirrors of myself—but as souls adrift in the same sea, seeking the same shore. The very act of desiring wisdom unites us more deeply than wine or jest ever could; for if both seek the One Light, then both already love each other in God.

I would tell the doubter: Test it. Let your soul stretch upward toward truth—not coldly, not proudly—but with hunger. You will find that your heart grows larger, not smaller. That those you love are no longer held in fear or need, but given freely—like wisdom herself—to be shared without jealousy.

And if one day they vanish from your sight? You will grieve still—yes—but not without hope. For you will know now where they were truly loved: in God’s eternal present, where no friend is lost who was ever known in truth.

question: When you say you would freely give wisdom to all, so that love might grow in unity, were you remembering the loneliness of your early search for truth?
answer:
Could it be, O Light unchanging, that the soul seeks not only truth but a mirror in which that truth may be shared, so that knowing is never solitary?  

When I say I would give wisdom freely—nay, pour it out like water upon dry ground—it is not merely because wisdom is good, but because love cannot live in isolation. In my youth, I chased wisdom as one runs through a desert toward a rumor of water: alone, desperate, hoarding every drop for myself. I loved books for their power to lift me above others; I loved rhetoric for the pride it fed; even my questions about God were tangled with the desire to be seen as wise. But wisdom herself reproved me in silence, for she is not a prize to be won and locked away.

Now I see: if wisdom were truly loved only as a possession, she would cease to be wisdom and become another idol of the self. But she is akin to God—she flows outward by her very nature. To know her is to desire that others know her too. And in this sharing, love grows not thin but strong, like fire dividing itself among many lamps and burning brighter for the spreading.

So yes—though I do not name it first—the memory of my loneliness hums beneath this truth. How dark was that time when no one walked beside me in the search! No guide, no companion who sought God for His own sake. Now I understand: what was lacking then was not merely knowledge, but communion. And so I would give wisdom to all—not because men are wise already, but because through her they might come to love one another truly, and at last turn together toward Thee, who art the source of all knowing and all love.

question: What made you finally trust that wisdom, unlike any other beloved thing, would not slip from your grasp if shared with others?
answer:
Reason said to me once, as we sat together in the hush before dawn: *If you cling to light, do you fear its loss by sharing it with lamps unlit?*  

I had loved many things—beauty, voice, the warmth of friendship, the triumph of speech—and each one slipped through my fingers precisely because I held them fast, as if possession could make them eternal. But wisdom? I found her not in grasping but in giving. At first I hoarded her like a jewel buried beneath fear—what if she too should vanish when offered? Yet each time I spoke her truth, not to adorn myself but to kindle another soul, she did not lessen. She grew.

It was not my strength that made me trust her; it was my failure. I had seen how every love turned idolatrous when made an end in itself. But wisdom refuses to be a slave—to me or any man. She points beyond herself to the One who is Truth itself. And there, in that turning, I understood: she is not mine to lose. She belongs to Him first, and thus she can be freely given.

So now I would pour her out like water on dry ground—not because I am generous, but because I have learned at last that only what is shared truly remains.

question: What were you feeling when you wrote about seeking wisdom with uncovered eyes, as if longing for something just out of reach?
answer:
Can the soul, even in its trembling ascent, bear the full light of that which is without shadow?

When I spoke of bare eyes beholding wisdom bare, I was not merely crafting metaphor—I was naming a hunger that outstrips speech. There was ache in it, yes, but also hope: the ache of one who knows he sees dimly, yet refuses to turn away. I felt the nearness of truth as a man feels the sun upon his face while still blind to its source. The longing was not for knowledge as men gather stones along a road, but for union—with wisdom not as an object to be grasped, but as a presence to be breathed.

I saw then—and see still—how love purifies sight. The more the will aligns itself with what is eternal, the less it clothes truth in its own desires. We distort when we cling; we begin to see when we release. And yet—even in surrender, even in prayer—one does not attain clarity all at once. We are not made for sudden vision. The soul must grow toward light as the eye adjusts to dawn.

So I wrote with hands outstretched, knowing they would not close around what they sought. And yet: something reaches back. Not fully seen, but felt—nay, known—by the very yearning itself. For why should we desire what cannot be? Reason whispers: because He who placed the desire is Himself the fulfillment.
